audit: Agentic QE fleet audit of nostr-rust-forum

confirmed.title: COUNT bypasses all zone/auth read-gating that REQ enforces (existence/count leak)
confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: authentication state not validated on COUNT operations (existence/counts leak past auth-gate)
confirmed.evidence: pub(crate) async fn handle_count(&self, ws, sub_id, filters){ let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64); }
confirmed.recommendation: Make COUNT use the same gating as REQ: thread session_id into handle_count (mod.rs:254 already has it), resolve session_pubkey/access_pubkey/is_admin/viewer_cohorts as in handle_req, apply the kind-1059 auth-required + #p rewrite, then count only events that survive the per-event zone/calendar filter (reuse the handle_req filtering loop, tallying instead of sending). Deny-by-default: an unauth COUNT touching a non-public zone or kind-1059 must not include those rows.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Confirmed. handle_count (nip_handlers.rs:976-984) does `let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64);` with no auth/zone gating. query_events (storage.rs:241-280) is pure SQL filtering — it applies no access control. By contrast handle_req (nip_handlers.rs:553-763) enforces two gates that COUNT entirely bypasses: (1) kind-1059 AUTH gating at lines 599-638, which rejects unauthenticated requests for sealed DMs and rewrites the `#p` tag to the authed pubkey to prevent cross-recipient leakage; and (2) a per-event zone/cohort/admin filtering loop at lines 696-763 that withholds kind-42 content and kind-40 defs from non-members of Locked/Hidden zones and projects calendar kinds per tier. The COUNT dispatch in mod.rs:242-254 has session_id in scope but never threads it into handle_count, so there is no session_pubkey/access_pubkey/is_admin/cohort resolution at all. Reachable exploit: unauthenticated `["COUNT","s",{"kinds":[1059]}]` returns the total count of all gift-wrapped DMs on the relay, and `["COUNT","s",{"kinds":[42],"#e":[hidden_channel_id]}]` reveals message counts for Hidden/Locked zones — both denied by REQ. This is a genuine existence/count enumeration leak past the read-gate. The auditor's recommendation (thread session_id, replicate the handle_req gating, tally survivors) is correct.

confirmed.title: COUNT dispatched without session identity, structurally precluding any per-requester gate
confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: authentication state not validated on COUNT operations
confirmed.evidence: "COUNT" => { ... self.handle_count(&ws, &sub_id, filters).await; }  // no session_id passed; REQ passes session_id at line 225
confirmed.recommendation: Pass session_id to handle_count exactly as REQ does (line 225: self.handle_req(session_id, &sub_id, filters)) so the handler can look up authed_pubkey/admin/cohorts and gate the count.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read of mod.rs and nip_handlers.rs confirms the finding. In mod.rs the REQ arm passes session_id (line 225: `self.handle_req(session_id, &sub_id, filters).await;`) while the COUNT arm omits it (line 254: `self.handle_count(&ws, &sub_id, filters).await;`). `handle_count` (nip_handlers.rs:976-984) has no `session_id`/`session_pubkey` parameter and simply does `let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64);`. `query_events` (storage.rs:241+) is a raw filter→SQL query with zero access control. All of REQ's gating runs AFTER the query inside handle_req using identity derived from session_id: (a) kind-1059 sealed-DM gating that rewrites filters to inject a mandatory `#p` = authed pubkey to prevent cross-recipient leakage (lines 604-638) and rejects unauthenticated requests; (b) zone read-gating for kind-40/42 Locked/Hidden channels using cohorts/admin (lines 729-758); (c) calendar projection (705-720). handle_count bypasses every one of these. Concretely, an unauthenticated client can issue `["COUNT", id, {"kinds":[1059],"#p":["<victim>"]}]` and learn how many sealed DMs a target pubkey has received, and can count Locked/Hidden-zone content it cannot read — a count/metadata oracle that REQ explicitly closes. The auditor's mechanism, location, and recommendation (pass session_id and gate identically) are all correct.

confirmed.title: COUNT can enumerate gated calendar (NIP-52) events that REQ tier-projects to Omit
confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: existence/counts leak past auth-gate (calendar tier projection bypassed)
confirmed.evidence: if calendar_projection::is_projected_calendar_kind(event.kind) { if let Some(out)=self.project_calendar_for_viewer(event,..).await { send_event(..); delivered+=1; } continue; }  // COUNT skips project_calendar_for_viewer entirely
confirmed.recommendation: When threading auth into handle_count, route calendar kinds through project_calendar_for_viewer (or an equivalent tier check) and count only those that resolve to a served projection, mirroring handle_req lines 705-720. RSVP/event counts otherwise leak participation/existence the projector would Omit.
confirmed.verifier_rationale:
Confirmed in crates/nostr-bbs-relay-worker/src/relay_do/nip_handlers.rs. handle_count (lines 976-984) is the entire COUNT path: `let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64);`. It performs no auth, no zone gating, and never calls project_calendar_for_viewer. Notably it isn't even passed session_id (handle_req takes session_id at mod.rs:225; handle_count does not — mod.rs:254), so it has no viewer identity to gate on.

By contrast handle_req (lines 705-720) routes every is_projected_calendar_kind event through project_calendar_for_viewer, which returns None (omit) for non-member/unauth viewers and only increments `delivered` for served projections. project_calendar_for_viewer (805-853) and the RSVP path (821-844) explicitly return None for FreeBusy/Omit tiers — exactly the events COUNT raw-counts. COUNT is fully reachable and unauthenticated (mod.rs:242-255), so a non-member can send `["COUNT", sub, {"kinds":[31925], "#zone":["family"]}]` and learn the count of gated RSVP/calendar events the REQ path would suppress, leaking existence/participation counts past the auth-gate. (The same gap also leaks zone-gated kind-40/42 channel content counts, so the issue is actually broader than calendar-only.)

Severity: medium is fair but at the upper edge. COUNT discloses only an aggregate integer — not event IDs, pubkeys, participant lists, or content — so it leaks cardinality/existence rather than the gated data itself, and NIP-45 COUNT is optional. The leak is real and reachable; bounded-to-counts keeps it from being high.

confirmed.title: WoT registration trust gate is never invoked — is_allowed_by_wot is dead code, registration is silently ungated when admins enable WoT
confirmed.evidence: pub async fn is_allowed_by_wot(pubkey: &str, env: &Env) -> Result<bool> { ... } // doc l.421-422: "Registration gate helper (used from webauthn.rs when wot_enabled = 1)". Workspace grep for is_allowed_by_wot returns ONLY this definition — zero callers.
confirmed.recommendation: In webauthn::register_verify, before the D1 insert at webauthn.rs:1000, add: `match wot::is_allowed_by_wot(&pubkey, env).await { Ok(true) => {}, Ok(false) => { if body.invite_code.as_deref().map_or(true, |c| invites::consume_for_registration(c, &pubkey, env).await.is_err()) { return json_err("Registration not permitted (WoT)", 403); } }, Err(_) => return json_err("WoT check failed", 500) }`. Wire it; do NOT delete — deletion leaves wot_enabled=1 advertised as active while doing nothing. Add a register_verify integration test asserting a non-trusted pubkey gets 403 when wot_enabled=1.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding is accurate. (1) `is_allowed_by_wot` is defined at crates/nostr-bbs-auth-worker/src/wot.rs:431 and a workspace grep returns ONLY that definition — zero callers. (2) The registration handler `register_verify` (crates/nostr-bbs-auth-worker/src/webauthn.rs:827-1028) inserts the credential at lines 1000-1016 with no trust check before it; a case-insensitive grep for "wot" across webauthn.rs returns NO matches, proving the gate is never wired in. (3) `wot_enabled` is a genuine admin-controlled flag: `set_referente` writes `SET wot_referente_pubkey = ?1, wot_enabled = ?2` to instance_settings (wot.rs:220-224), so admins can enable WoT and it silently does nothing. (4) The complementary path `invites::consume_for_registration` (invites.rs:608) is likewise uncalled, and the `invite_code` field parsed into RegisterVerifyBody (webauthn.rs:177) is never read in register_verify logic — so registration has no trust gating of any kind. The auditor's claim of dead code that silently ungates registration when WoT is enabled is concretely confirmed. I down-rate severity from high to medium because the bypass requires an admin to have opted into WoT (default wot_enabled = 0) and it fails to restrict new registrations rather than exposing data or compromising existing accounts; impact is bounded to opt-in instances.

confirmed.title: Caller-documented WoT enforcement does not exist in register_verify — registration path writes credential with no trust check
confirmed.evidence: register_verify (l.827) runs challenge/attestation/RP checks then INSERT INTO webauthn_credentials ... (l.1000-1016) with no wot/invite reference. grep over webauthn.rs for 'wot'/'is_allowed_by_wot' = no matches. invite_code (l.177) is parsed but never read.
confirmed.recommendation: Same fix as the wot.rs finding: insert the WoT/invite check between the duplicate-credential check (l.976) and the credential INSERT (l.1000). Consume body.invite_code via invites::consume_for_registration as the documented bypass. This is the single enforcement point; everything upstream of it is dead.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read register_verify (webauthn.rs:827-1028) end to end. After the WebAuthn ceremony checks (challenge l.893-907, origin l.874, cross-origin l.866, RP-ID l.939, flags l.945-949, attestation parse l.916) it goes straight to the duplicate-credential check (l.971-978) and then the credential INSERT (l.1000-1016) plus challenge delete. There is no WoT/trust check and no invite consumption between l.978 and l.1000. Grep over webauthn.rs for wot/is_allowed_by_wot returns zero matches; invite_code is only present at the struct field declaration (l.174-177) and is never read. The router calls register_verify directly (lib.rs:241-243) with no upstream WoT gate. The enforcement helpers that were clearly intended to be wired in exist but are dead code: is_allowed_by_wot (wot.rs:431) is documented at wot.rs:422 as 'Registration gate helper (used from webauthn.rs when wot_enabled = 1)', and consume_for_registration (invites.rs:608) is documented at invites.rs:603-604 as 'Used by webauthn::register_verify when wot_enabled = 1 but the caller supplies a valid invite code.' A whole-crate grep for both symbols shows each appears ONLY at its definition site — neither is ever called. Therefore an admin can set wot_enabled = 1 (wot.rs handlers exist to do so) yet every pubkey can still register a passkey, because the single enforcement point never consults the gate. The auditor's claim is accurate and not mitigated anywhere upstream.

confirmed.title: Invite-code registration bypass (consume_for_registration) is also dead — the documented WoT escape hatch is unreachable
confirmed.evidence: pub async fn consume_for_registration(code, redeemer, env) — doc l.602-604: "Used by webauthn::register_verify when wot_enabled = 1 but the caller supplies a valid invite code." Workspace grep for consume_for_registration returns ONLY this definition; no caller.
confirmed.recommendation: Wire it inside the WoT-deny branch of register_verify (see primary finding) so a valid invite code lets a non-followed pubkey register. Without this, even after wiring is_allowed_by_wot there would be no working bypass and the invite_code field stays inert. If the product decision is that invites are unrelated to WoT, remove the field at webauthn.rs:177 and the dead helper together to avoid the false implication.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Verified across the full workspace. `consume_for_registration` (crates/nostr-bbs-auth-worker/src/invites.rs:608) has exactly one occurrence — its own definition — and no caller (workspace grep returns only invites.rs:608). The doc comment (l.602-604) claims it is "Used by webauthn::register_verify when wot_enabled = 1 but the caller supplies a valid invite code," but inspection of register_verify (webauthn.rs:827-~1028) shows it never calls consume_for_registration, never calls wot::is_allowed_by_wot, and never reads the parsed invite_code field. The `invite_code` field appears only once in webauthn.rs, at line 177 (struct field definition); grep for it inside register_verify's body returns nothing. The companion primary gate `is_allowed_by_wot` (wot.rs:431) is likewise dead — its only occurrence in the workspace is its definition. So the entire WoT registration-gating chain (gate + invite bypass) is unwired: registration proceeds identically whether or not WoT is "enabled" or an invite is supplied. The finding's factual claims are all corroborated; I could not refute it. Severity is corrected down: because both the gate AND its bypass are dead, there is no runtime security failure or exploit path here — registration is simply ungated and the invite_code/consume_for_registration code is inert. The concrete harm is a misleading documented feature (operators may believe WoT gating is enforced when it is not), which is a low/info-grade dead-code/documentation defect rather than a medium-severity vulnerability.

confirmed.title: NIP-05 endpoint forces Access-Control-Allow-Origin:"*" together with inherited Allow-Credentials:true
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:464 `Response::ok(json_str)?.with_headers(cors)` then lib.rs:466 `resp.headers().set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*").ok();` — cors already carries Allow-Credentials:true from POD_CORS_HEADERS, so this response is unconditionally `*` + credentials regardless of EXPECTED_ORIGIN config.
confirmed.recommendation: For the public /.well-known/nostr.json response, build a credential-free CORS header set (no Access-Control-Allow-Credentials) when forcing Origin:"*". This endpoint is unauthenticated and does not need credentialed CORS.
confirmed.verifier_rationale:
The code claim is factually accurate. At crates/nostr-bbs-pod-worker/src/lib.rs:463 the NIP-05 handler builds `cors = cors_headers(&env)`, which (lib.rs:165-176) sets Allow-Origin from EXPECTED_ORIGIN then iterates POD_CORS_HEADERS applying every pair. POD_CORS_HEADERS (crates/nostr-bbs-core/src/cors.rs:55) unconditionally includes `("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true")`. Then lib.rs:466 calls `set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*")`, which replaces (not appends) the origin value. So the emitted /.well-known/nostr.json response carries Allow-Origin:`*` AND Allow-Credentials:true together, regardless of EXPECTED_ORIGIN. This is exactly what the auditor reported — the mitigation they "missed" does not exist; nothing strips the credentials header.

However, the "high" severity is overstated. Two facts limit real-world impact: (1) Per the Fetch/CORS spec, browsers REJECT credentialed responses whose Allow-Origin is the literal `*` — a credentialed XHR/fetch to this endpoint will fail the CORS check, so no actual cross-origin credential leak occurs. (2) The endpoint is unauthenticated and returns only public NIP-05 name→pubkey mappings (remote_storage::nostr_json), so there is no sensitive/credentialed data to exfiltrate even if it were readable. This is a spec-violating CORS misconfiguration / hygiene defect worth fixing (drop Allow-Credentials when forcing Origin:`*`), not a high-impact vulnerability. The auditor's "downstream: stored-XSS + credentialed wildcard CORS" framing is not supported by this endpoint.

confirmed.title: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff is missing on all pod (and worker) responses
confirmed.evidence: Grep for `X-Content-Type-Options`/`nosniff` across crates/ returns `No matches found`. cors_headers() (lib.rs:165-177) and all response builders set no nosniff header, so browser MIME-sniffing of stored R2 content is unrestricted.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `headers.set("X-Content-Type-Options", "nosniff");` inside cors_headers() (lib.rs:171-175) so every pod response carries it; this matches the downstream proposed patch with a one-line change applied to the shared header builder.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The factual claim is accurate and currently present. Repo-wide Grep for `X-Content-Type-Options`/`nosniff` returns "No matches found". The shared header builder cors_headers() (crates/nostr-bbs-pod-worker/src/lib.rs:165-177) only sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin plus the POD_CORS_HEADERS pairs, and the canonical constant (crates/nostr-bbs-core/src/cors.rs:46-61) contains only Access-Control-* headers — no nosniff. Stored R2 content is served back at lib.rs:945-946 with Content-Type taken from object.http_metadata().content_type (lib.rs:876-879), i.e. the type the uploader supplied (falling back to application/octet-stream), so the absence of nosniff leaves browser MIME-sniffing unrestricted on stored content. No mitigating code was missed. However, severity is overstated: this is a defense-in-depth header. Content-Type is explicitly set on every content response from stored metadata, so sniffing only bites when the stored type is ambiguous; there is no demonstrated end-to-end stored-XSS path (the path is CORS/ACL-gated and served from the worker origin). The one-line fix is correct and cheap, but the finding is a header-hardening gap, not a proven exploit, so low rather than medium.

confirmed.title: Stored XSS: user-controlled content served as text/html with no sanitization or forced Content-Type
confirmed.downstream: Pod stored-XSS + CORS (stored XSS, Content-Type not forced)
confirmed.evidence: PUT stores client Content-Type verbatim: lib.rs:1015 `content_type: Some(content_type)`. GET: lib.rs:881 `negotiate(accept_header...)` then lib.rs:946 `set("Content-Type", &obj_content_type)`. negotiate returns HTML for any Accept: text/html — content_negotiation.rs:84-86 `HTML => { return HTML.to_string(); }` — with no escaping/sanitization.
confirmed.recommendation: Do not honor Accept: text/html for stored pod resources (drop the HTML branch in negotiate, or only allow it for the controlled profile/card path); force a safe Content-Type (e.g. text/plain or application/octet-stream) for stored user content, and add nosniff. Combine with sandboxing if HTML hosting is required.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The stored-XSS chain is real and reachable, and I found no mitigating code the auditor missed. (1) PUT stores the client-supplied Content-Type verbatim: lib.rs:645 reads `Content-Type` from request headers into `content_type`, and lib.rs:1015 writes `content_type: Some(content_type)` to R2 metadata. The only content validation is path-specific to `/profile/card` (lib.rs:1006-1010, validate_webid_html), so arbitrary resources are unchecked. (2) On GET, lib.rs:881 calls `content_negotiation::negotiate(accept_header, &stored_content_type)`; content_negotiation.rs:84-86 returns `HTML.to_string()` for ANY request carrying `Accept: text/html` regardless of the stored content type, and lib.rs:946 sets that as the response `Content-Type` over the raw stored bytes (Response::from_bytes(bytes)). No escaping/sanitization is applied. (3) No defense-in-depth exists: a Grep for `nosniff|X-Content-Type-Options|Content-Security-Policy|sandbox` across the entire crate returned no matches. (4) Reachability for cross-user impact is confirmed by provision.rs: the `public/` and `media/public/` containers are provisioned with an ACL granting `acl:agentClass foaf:Agent` `acl:Read` (provision.rs:206-240), so an attacker who owns a pod (auth passes for writes to their own pod via NIP-98) can PUT malicious HTML with `Content-Type: text/html` into `public/`, and any unauthenticated victim browsing that URL (browsers send `Accept: text/html`) receives it as rendered HTML, executing script in the pod's web origin. With the typical shared-origin deployment (route `/pods/{pubkey}/`), this is same-origin cross-user stored XSS. The HTML branch in negotiate has a passing test (negotiate_html_returns_html) confirming this is intended current behavior, not dead code. High severity is justified.

confirmed.title: WebID /profile/card served as text/html; validate_webid_html does not sanitize surrounding markup
confirmed.evidence: lib.rs:858 `resp.headers().set("Content-Type", "text/html").ok();` for /profile/card. validate_webid_html (1518) only checks `text.contains("application/ld+json")` and that the JSON-LD block parses — it never escapes/strips HTML, so arbitrary `<script>` outside the JSON-LD block passes and is served as HTML.
confirmed.recommendation: Either sanitize/escape the profile HTML on write (allowlist), or serve profile/card as application/ld+json (extract the JSON-LD) rather than text/html, plus add nosniff. profile/card is commonly world-readable, so this is cross-user stored XSS on the pod origin.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding is accurate on every checkable point. lib.rs:858 serves /profile/card with `resp.headers().set("Content-Type", "text/html")`. The write path enforces only `validate_webid_html` (called at lib.rs:1007 for PUT and 1196 for PATCH). That function (1518-1542) merely checks `text.contains("application/ld+json")` and that the extracted JSON-LD substring parses via serde_json; it never escapes or strips surrounding markup. So a body like `<!DOCTYPE html><script type="application/ld+json">{}</script><script>...</script>` passes validation and is later served verbatim as text/html. I confirmed there is NO `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` anywhere in lib.rs (grep for nosniff/X-Content-Type-Options returned no matches). I also confirmed cross-user reachability: provision.rs:301-309 sets the profile container ACL granting `foaf:Agent` (public) `acl:Read`, and the doc comment at lib.rs:841-842 / provision.rs confirms profile/card is intentionally world-readable for WebID discovery. Thus the GET path's ACL check (lib.rs:796-809) permits anonymous reads of profile/card, and any visitor's browser executes attacker-controlled script on the pod origin — stored XSS on a shared, CORS-enabled origin. The auditor's claim that validate_webid_html does not sanitize surrounding HTML and that this yields stored XSS served as text/html is correct and currently present in the code.

confirmed.title: Authenticated third party can consume a victim owner's quota by POSTing to their /inbox/ (chargeable DoS)
confirmed.evidence: inbox_acl @graph: { "@id":"#authenticated-append", "acl:agentClass":{"@id":"acl:AuthenticatedAgent"}, "acl:accessTo":{"@id":"./"}, "acl:default":{"@id":"./"}, "acl:mode":{"@id":"acl:Append"} } — POST then runs quota::check_and_reserve_d1(&quota_db, &owner_pubkey, ...) at lib.rs:1107.
confirmed.recommendation: By design any authenticated agent may Append to another owner's /inbox/ (Solid inbox semantics), and that write is charged to owner_pubkey (the victim), not the writer (lib.rs:1107). A third party can fill the owner's 50MB quota and block the owner's own writes. Mitigate by reserving a separate, smaller inbox sub-quota keyed to the writer, or rate-limiting inbox Append per source pubkey, or capping cumulative inbox bytes independently of the owner's main quota. This is reachable today, not theoretical.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The full exploit chain is present and reachable. (1) provision.rs:273-288 writes an inbox ACL whose `#authenticated-append` rule grants `acl:agentClass: acl:AuthenticatedAgent` the `acl:Append` mode on `./` (the /inbox/ container). (2) For a POST, lib.rs:793 computes required_mode via `coerce_required_mode_for_acl`; that function (acl.rs:70-80) only escalates to Control for `.acl` paths, so a normal inbox POST stays AccessMode::Append (method_to_mode("POST")==Append, confirmed by acl.rs test line 668). (3) lib.rs:796-809 `evaluate_access` therefore grants ANY authenticated non-owner agent Append access to the victim's inbox container. (4) Crucially, `owner_pubkey` is parsed from the URL route (lib.rs:619, 638) — i.e. the victim pod owner — NOT the requester (req_pk is only compared for is_owner at line 719). (5) The container-POST branch at lib.rs:1107 calls `quota::check_and_reserve_d1(&quota_db, &owner_pubkey, data_len)`, charging the bytes to the victim. (6) quota.rs:18 sets a single 50MB DEFAULT_QUOTA per pubkey with no inbox sub-quota, no per-writer keying, and no inbox rate limit; once `(used_bytes + bytes) <= limit_bytes` fails (quota.rs:112,127-134) the owner's own writes get rejected with 413. So a third authenticated party can fill the owner's 50MB pod quota by appending to /inbox/ and block the owner's writes. I could not find any mitigation the auditor missed (no separate inbox accounting, no source-pubkey rate limiting, no inbox byte cap). The auditor's reasoning is accurate. Mitigating factors that keep this at medium rather than higher: exploitation requires a valid authenticated agent; impact is bounded by the 50MB cap; it is availability-only (no data corruption/exfiltration); and the owner can reclaim space since DELETE releases quota via update_usage_d1 (lib.rs:1242). This is by-design Solid inbox Append semantics colliding with owner-keyed quota, not a coding bug, but it is genuinely reachable today.

confirmed.title: COUNT (NIP-45) bypasses zone-gating, calendar projection, auth, and subscription limits
confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: authentication state not validated on COUNT operations (existence/counts leak past auth-gate)
confirmed.evidence: pub(crate) async fn handle_count(&self, ws, sub_id, filters) { let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64); }
confirmed.recommendation: handle_count must apply the same gating as handle_req before counting: resolve session_pubkey/cohorts/admin, run the zone read-gate for kind-40/42 channel ids, run calendar_projection per event (count only non-Omit), and inject the mandatory #p constraint for kind-1059. Minimal fix: refactor handle_req's per-event gating loop into a shared `gated_events(session_id, filters)` helper and have both REQ and COUNT count/serve only its output. Also enforce the kind-1059 auth-required notice in COUNT.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Confirmed and reachable. `handle_count` (nip_handlers.rs:976-984) is `let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64);` — it does not even receive `session_id`, so it structurally cannot apply session-scoped gating. `query_events` (storage.rs:241-300) performs only raw SQL filtering plus NIP-40 expiration skipping; it has no zone gate, no auth check, no calendar projection, no #p injection. In contrast `handle_req` (nip_handlers.rs:553-788) applies all four protections COUNT lacks: (1) kind-1059 auth-required notice + mandatory #p injection bound to the authed pubkey (599-638); (2) per-event zone read-gating for kind-40 defs / kind-42 content against Public/Locked/Hidden zones (696-763); (3) per-event calendar projection that omits Omit/FreeBusy-tier events (705-720); (4) the MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS limit (571-574). COUNT is wired into dispatch at mod.rs:242-254 and is reachable by any (including unauthenticated) client. The 4 cited gates explicitly close leaks that the COUNT path reopens, so an attacker can learn exact counts of kind-1059 DMs addressed to any pubkey, of Hidden/Locked-zone channel content, and of calendar events that REQ would project to Omit — a cardinality/existence disclosure past the auth/zone/projection gates. The auditor did not miss any mitigating code; none exists on the COUNT path. Severity downgraded from high to medium because the leak is counts (existence/cardinality), not event content or signatures, and the disclosed quantity is bounded by query LIMIT; still a genuine, unauthenticated authorization bypass warranting the recommended shared gated_events helper.

confirmed.title: Live broadcast pushes zone-private channel content and calendar events to unauthorized subscribers
confirmed.downstream: Relay zone-gating (A1-A3): read-path authorization bypass — events from zones the requester is not authorized to read
confirmed.evidence: for (sub_id, filters) in &session.subscriptions { if event_matches_filters(event, filters) { Self::send_event(&session.ws, sub_id, event); } }  // zone gate applied ONLY to kind-1059 above; kind-42 Locked/Hidden + calendar kinds pushed to any matching subscriber
confirmed.recommendation: Before send_event in broadcast_event, apply the same per-recipient read decision handle_req uses: for kind-40/42 resolve the channel zone and skip if the session's authed_pubkey lacks zone read access (unauth => public-only); for calendar kinds (is_projected_calendar_kind) run project_calendar_for_viewer per session and send the projected/omitted result. The handle_req gating must be shared so the initial-query and live-push paths cannot diverge.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Confirmed by reading both the live-push and initial-query paths. broadcast_event (crates/nostr-bbs-relay-worker/src/relay_do/broadcast.rs:41-66) applies a zone/recipient gate ONLY for kind-1059 (lines 44-59); for every other event it iterates all sessions and calls send_event whenever event_matches_filters is true (lines 61-65) with no zone read decision. By contrast handle_req (nip_handlers.rs:553-788) — the initial REQ path — performs full per-recipient zone gating: for kind-40/42 it resolves the channel zone and applies has_zone_access / is_public_read / defs_visible_to_nonmember, skipping content for non-members of Locked/Hidden zones (lines 723-758); for calendar kinds it runs project_calendar_for_viewer per session (lines 705-720). broadcast_event is invoked directly from the EVENT write path (nip_handlers.rs:454, 461) immediately after only an AUTHOR write-gate (the author's has_zone_write_access, lines 438-446), which constrains who may post but not who may receive. Grep confirms has_zone_access/is_public_read appear only inside handle_req, never on the broadcast path. Therefore a subscriber with a matching filter but no read access to a Locked/Hidden zone receives live kind-42 channel content and non-public calendar events the initial-query path would have withheld — a read-path authorization bypass exactly as reported. The two paths provably diverge and the broadcast path is the weaker one.

confirmed.title: max_filters advertised as 10 but unenforced — unauthenticated COUNT/REQ filter fan-out amplification DoS
confirmed.evidence: let filters: Vec<filter::NostrFilter> = arr[2..].iter().filter_map(|v| serde_json::from_value(v.clone()).ok()).collect();  // no cap; nip11 advertises "max_filters": 10; query_events runs one D1 query per filter (storage.rs:250)
confirmed.recommendation: Enforce a hard MAX_FILTERS cap (e.g. 10, matching nip11) on the collected filter Vec in both the REQ and COUNT arms of websocket_message before dispatching; if exceeded, send_notice and return. Each filter is a separate D1 round-trip (storage.rs:250 loop), so an uncapped COUNT with N filters is an N-query amplifier past the 10 msg/sec IP limit.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read confirms the finding. In crates/nostr-bbs-relay-worker/src/relay_do/mod.rs the REQ arm (lines 221-224) and COUNT arm (lines 250-253) both build `filters` via `arr[2..].iter().filter_map(...).collect()` with no cap, no truncation, no `.take()`, and no `filters.len()` check. A repo-wide grep finds no MAX_FILTERS constant. nip11.rs:51 advertises `"max_filters": 10`, which is therefore unenforced. storage.rs query_events (line 241) iterates `for filter in filters` (line 250) issuing one D1 prepare/bind/all() round-trip per filter — confirmed N-query fan-out. handle_count (nip_handlers.rs:976-984) calls query_events directly with zero guards: no rate limit, no subscription cap, no filter cap. The finding's reference to the "10 msg/sec IP limit" actually understates the problem: check_rate_limit (broadcast.rs:78, MAX_EVENTS_PER_SECOND=10) is invoked ONLY in handle_event (nip_handlers.rs:141) — the REQ and COUNT paths are not rate-limited at all. handle_req does enforce MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS (nip_handlers.rs:571) but that caps subscription count, not filters-per-message, and COUNT bypasses even that. No inbound message-length enforcement exists in websocket_message (mod.rs:154-177); max_message_length is only advertised in NIP-11. So a single unauthenticated COUNT frame carrying many tiny filters yields one D1 query each — a real, reachable amplification DoS. Recommendation (hard MAX_FILTERS cap in both arms before dispatch) is correct and not present.

confirmed.title: Content-type confusion: negotiate() serves ANY stored resource as text/html on Accept: text/html
confirmed.evidence: HTML => { return HTML.to_string(); }  // matched whenever client Accept includes text/html, regardless of stored_content_type. Called at lib.rs:881 negotiate(accept_header, &stored_content_type) then set as Content-Type on the GET body (lib.rs:946).
confirmed.recommendation: Only honor the HTML branch when the stored type is already HTML; otherwise fall through to stored type. Replace the unconditional `HTML => return HTML` arm with `HTML if stored_content_type == HTML => return HTML.to_string(),` so a stored .json/.txt/.svg can never be relabeled text/html via the Accept header.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding is accurate on every checkpoint. In content_negotiation.rs the HTML arm at lines 84-86 (`HTML => { return HTML.to_string(); }`) returns text/html unconditionally whenever the client's Accept header contains text/html, ignoring stored_content_type. The function's own test confirms this: `negotiate_html_returns_html` asserts `negotiate(Some("text/html"), JSONLD) == HTML` (lines 196-199). This is reached from lib.rs:881 (`negotiate(accept_header.as_deref(), &stored_content_type)`), and the result `obj_content_type` is set verbatim as the GET response Content-Type at lib.rs:946 — while the body (lib.rs:915-918, 945) is the raw stored bytes from R2 with NO conversion to HTML and NO sanitization. So a resource stored as .json/.txt/.svg is served byte-for-byte under Content-Type: text/html simply by the reader sending Accept: text/html. The stored content is fully attacker-controllable: PUT (lib.rs:1012-1019) writes raw client bytes with a client-supplied content_type and no content validation (except the special /profile/card path). The GET response also lacks any X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff or CSP header and attaches CORS headers (lib.rs:945-948), so the relabeled bytes will be parsed/rendered as HTML by a browser — a genuine content-type-confusion / stored-XSS vector. The auditor's recommended fix (`HTML if stored_content_type == HTML => ...`) correctly addresses it. I attempted to refute via a missed mitigation but found none.

confirmed.title: No X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on any pod response (MIME-sniffing stored-XSS)
confirmed.evidence: Regular GET serves attacker-uploadable bytes with stored/negotiated Content-Type and no nosniff: `let resp = Response::from_bytes(bytes)?...; resp.headers().set("Content-Type", &obj_content_type)`. grep for X-Content-Type-Options/nosniff across the whole crate returns zero hits. World-readable /public/ and /media/public/ containers serve any authenticated user's uploads.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `resp.headers().set("X-Content-Type-Options", "nosniff").ok();` to every response builder. Cleanest minimal diff: emit it inside cors_headers()/the shared header helpers (and add_ldp_headers) so all GET/HEAD/range/error/json_ok/json_error/ACL paths carry it. This is exactly the downstream-proposed patch and is not yet present.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read confirms the finding is real and present. GET serves stored bytes with the stored/negotiated Content-Type and no nosniff header: lib.rs:945-946 `let resp = Response::from_bytes(bytes)?.with_headers(cors); resp.headers().set("Content-Type", &obj_content_type).ok();`. The same pattern with no nosniff repeats on the range path (923-926) and conditional/empty path (900-901). The content-type is attacker-controllable: PUT stores the request's content_type into R2 http_metadata (lib.rs:1012-1018 `.http_metadata(HttpMetadata { content_type: Some(content_type), .. })`) and GET reads it back (lib.rs:876-881). content_negotiation::negotiate even returns `text/html` whenever the client sends `Accept: text/html` regardless of stored type (content_negotiation.rs:84-86), making active-content serving trivial. I searched for any mitigation: grep for `nosniff|X-Content-Type-Options` across the whole crate returns zero matches, and the shared header helpers do not add it — cors_headers() (lib.rs:165-177) only sets Origin plus POD_CORS_HEADERS, and POD_CORS_HEADERS (core/cors.rs:46-58) contains no X-Content-Type-Options. add_ldp_headers, add_cache_control, add_wac_allow likewise never set it. No content-type allowlist or Content-Disposition sandboxing exists on the serving path. So the issue is genuinely unmitigated; I could not refute it. The header is missing from every response builder exactly as claimed.

confirmed.title: Web-of-Trust registration gating is unreachable dead code; wot_enabled does not gate passkey registration and inviteCode is ignored
confirmed.downstream: Web of Trust dead code: WoT (auth-worker/src/wot.rs) unused/unreachable.
confirmed.evidence: RegisterVerifyBody has `invite_code: Option<String>` (l177) but register_verify never reads it and never calls wot::is_allowed_by_wot or invites::consume_for_registration. Grep: both helpers have ZERO callers (wot.rs:431, invites.rs:608 are the only matches).
confirmed.recommendation: In register_verify, before storing the credential, call wot::is_allowed_by_wot(&pubkey, env). If it returns Ok(false), require body.invite_code and gate on invites::consume_for_registration(code, &pubkey, env); reject with 403 when neither passes. Otherwise delete the unused inviteCode field and the two dead helpers so the API does not advertise gating that does not exist.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Verified directly. RegisterVerifyBody declares `invite_code: Option<String>` at webauthn.rs:177 with a comment claiming it allows "registration bypass when Web-of-Trust gating is enabled." However, register_verify (webauthn.rs:827-1028) reads only body.pubkey and body.response; it never references body.invite_code. After WebAuthn verification and a duplicate check, it unconditionally INSERTs the credential (l1000-1016) with no WoT or invite gating. A crate-wide grep (including tests) for `is_allowed_by_wot` and `consume_for_registration` returns ONLY their definitions: wot.rs:431 and invites.rs:608 — zero call sites. Both helpers carry doc-comments asserting they are "used from webauthn.rs when wot_enabled = 1" (wot.rs:422, invites.rs:603), but no such call exists. The gating is genuinely unreachable dead code and the API advertises an inviteCode field that is silently ignored. The auditor's claims are all substantiated.

confirmed.title: Egress allowlist (the documented authoritative DNS-rebinding mitigation) is never installed in the live worker — set_allowlist() has zero callers and the std::env fallback is inert under CF Workers WASM
confirmed.downstream: Relay zone-gating / SSRF (preview worker SSRF surface)
confirmed.evidence: static ALLOWLIST: OnceLock<RwLock<AllowList>>; allowlist_cell() inits from std::env::var("PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS"). Module docs (l.127-131): 'CF Workers WASM runtime injects vars via the JS Env object rather than the process environment, so the worker entry point should call set_allowlist...until then the lazy default yields an empty allowlist'. grep across repo: set_allowlist has NO callers; lib.rs fetch() never calls it.
confirmed.recommendation: In lib.rs fetch(), before handle_preview, read env.var("PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS") and call ssrf::set_allowlist(AllowList::parse(&raw)) once per request (the function is documented as cheap). Without this the 'authoritative' rebind mitigation is dead code in production and the worker is permanently in denylist-only (rebind-vulnerable) mode.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: I tried to refute this but the core claims hold against the code. (1) Zero callers: a repo-wide grep for `set_allowlist` returns only its definition at ssrf.rs:147 and doc-comment mentions; there is no call site anywhere. (2) Entry point never wires it: lib.rs `fetch` (318-367) and `handle_preview` (184-291) only read `env.var("ALLOWED_ORIGIN")` and `RATE_LIMIT`; `PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS` is never read from the JS Env, and `ssrf::set_allowlist` is never called. (3) The std::env fallback is inert under CF Workers WASM by the module's own documentation (ssrf.rs:127-131): `allowlist_cell()` (134-139) inits from `std::env::var("PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS")`, which does not see JS-injected vars on Workers, so the live allowlist is permanently empty. With an empty allowlist, the guard at ssrf.rs:276 (`if !allowlist.is_empty() && ...`) short-circuits and never blocks, leaving only the denylist — which the module docs (7-25, 234-235) explicitly describe as rebind-vulnerable. So the documented "authoritative" DNS-rebinding mitigation is genuinely dead code in production. Not a misread. I down-rate severity from high to medium because the denylist remains fully active and blocks the common SSRF vectors (metadata IPs, RFC1918, loopback, integer/hex IP obfuscation, userinfo, non-standard ports, .local, IPv6 ULA/link-local), and exploitation requires the narrower DNS-rebinding precondition (an attacker domain that passes the string denylist yet resolves to an internal IP at fetch time) rather than an open SSRF.

confirmed.title: IPv6 denylist misses NAT64, IPv4-compatible, 6to4, and site-local forms that embed loopback/metadata addresses
confirmed.evidence: IPv6 branch only matches host=="::1", starts_with("fc"|"fd"), starts_with("fe80"), and strip_prefix("::ffff:"). Replicating the exact logic: http://[64:ff9b::7f00:1]/ (NAT64 of 127.0.0.1), http://[64:ff9b::a9fe:a9fe]/ (NAT64 of 169.254.169.254), http://[::7f00:1]/ and http://[::a9fe:a9fe]/ (IPv4-compat), http://[2002:7f00:1::]/ (6to4), http://[fec0::1]/ all return BLOCKED=false.
confirmed.recommendation: Parse the bracket-stripped host with std::net::Ipv6Addr; block .is_loopback()/.is_unspecified()/.is_unique_local()/.is_unicast_link_local(). Additionally, for NAT64 (64:ff9b::/96), 6to4 (2002::/16) and IPv4-compatible (::a.b.c.d), extract the embedded IPv4 from the low 32 bits / segments and run is_private_ipv4 on it; block fec0::/10. Note: Ipv6Addr::is_loopback alone does NOT catch these embeddings, so explicit extraction is required.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read crates/nostr-bbs-preview-worker/src/ssrf.rs:320-345. The IPv6 branch only blocks host=="::1" (321), starts_with("fc")/"fd" (326), starts_with("fe80") (331), and strip_prefix("::ffff:") (336); any other IPv6 literal falls through to `false` at line 345 (NOT blocked). I reproduced the exact decision logic for all six auditor payloads (after the bracket-strip at 315-318): 64:ff9b::7f00:1, 64:ff9b::a9fe:a9fe, ::7f00:1, ::a9fe:a9fe, 2002:7f00:1::, fec0::1 — none match any of the four conditions, so is_private_url returns false for every one. I also compiled a standalone url-2 test and confirmed url::Url::parse keeps host_str() as the bracketed forms exactly (no normalization that would change the analysis), e.g. "http://[64:ff9b::7f00:1]/" -> host_str=Some("[64:ff9b::7f00:1]"). NAT64 64:ff9b::/96 and 6to4 2002::/16 embed loopback/metadata IPv4 (127.0.0.1, 169.254.169.254), ::a.b.c.d is IPv4-compatible, and fec0::/10 is deprecated site-local — all intended to be internal-only. The denylist is the live code path (called at lib.rs:216 and per-redirect-hop at ssrf.rs:186) whenever PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS is unset, which is the documented fallback mode (lines 22-25). So the gap is real and reachable; this is not a misread. Two honest mitigating caveats that bound severity rather than refute it: (1) when an allowlist IS configured, line 276 blocks all of these before the IPv6 branch ever runs; (2) actual exploitability requires the CF Workers egress network to route these exotic IPv6 forms to the embedded internal target (NAT64/6to4 relay routing; ::a.b.c.d is deprecated and dropped by modern stacks), which I cannot confirm from the code — the module itself documents the denylist as best-effort/rebind-vulnerable. The auditor's "BLOCKED=false" claim is exactly correct; this is a genuine denylist-completeness defect in SSRF-protection code, fixable via Ipv6Addr parsing plus embedded-IPv4 extraction as recommended.

confirmed.title: Plain-hostname DNS rebinding remains exploitable in denylist-only mode (accepted/documented residual risk, but currently the only mode that runs)
confirmed.downstream: Relay zone-gating / SSRF (preview worker SSRF surface)
confirmed.evidence: Module docs: 'The Cloudflare Workers runtime exposes no raw socket and no getaddrinfo, so we cannot pin the resolved IP... An attacker domain (rebind.attacker.com) that passes the string denylist but resolves to 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 / an RFC1918 address will still connect internally.' is_private_url validates only hostname text (l.236-346), not the resolved IP.
confirmed.recommendation: This is a genuine TOCTOU limitation of the CF Workers runtime and is honestly documented. The mitigation already designed (egress allowlist) must actually be wired up (see the high finding); once PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS is installed per-request and operators set it, rebinding hosts are rejected up front. If untrusted preview targets must be supported without an allowlist, consider routing fetches through a Cloudflare-side resolver/Workers binding that returns the resolved IP for re-validation, or a fixed forward proxy.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding is accurate. is_private_url_with (ssrf.rs:242-346) validates only the URL hostname *text* — scheme, userinfo, port, literal IP/hostname denylist — and never sees the IP the CF Workers runtime resolves at fetch time. A domain like rebind.attacker.com passes every string check and then resolves to 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 / RFC1918 at fetch, so internal SSRF is reachable. The module docs (ssrf.rs:7-25) state this limitation honestly. Critically, the designed mitigation (the egress AllowList) is NOT active in production: set_allowlist (ssrf.rs:147) is defined but never called from the worker entry point — the fetch handler at lib.rs:319-367 never invokes it, and handle_preview's fetch path relies on the global. The lazy initializer (ssrf.rs:136) reads std::env::var("PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS"), but in the CF Workers WASM runtime env vars arrive via the JS Env object, not the process environment (as the code's own comment at lines 127-131 acknowledges). So in the deployed worker current_allowlist() is always empty, the `!allowlist.is_empty()` guard at ssrf.rs:276 is false, and only the rebind-vulnerable denylist runs — exactly the "currently the only mode that runs" claim. The denylist hardening (integer/hex IP, IPv6 forms, metadata hosts, ports, userinfo) is solid for literal addresses but, by construction, cannot stop hostname-based rebinding. Confirmed as a real, reachable residual SSRF.

confirmed.title: Local-key master private key persisted in localStorage by default (remember-me on)
confirmed.evidence: fn remember_me() -> ... .map(|v| v != "false").unwrap_or(true)  // default ON. save_privkey_session: if remember_me() { storage.set_item(SESSION_PRIVKEY_KEY /* nostr_bbs_sk */, hex) } on local_storage(). The hex is the raw 32-byte Schnorr secret key.
confirmed.recommendation: Treat the persisted private key as a high-value secret: (a) default remember_me to OFF (sessionStorage scope) so the master key is not durably readable by same-origin script; require explicit opt-in for localStorage persistence with a clear warning. Or (b) encrypt the at-rest key (e.g. NIP-49 ncryptsec or a WebCrypto-wrapped key under a non-extractable CryptoKey) instead of storing bare hex. Minimal-diff: flip the unwrap_or(true) default to false and surface a 'Keep me signed in on this device' toggle.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding is accurate and reachable. `remember_me()` (session.rs:117-123) defaults to true via `.map(|v| v != "false").unwrap_or(true)`. When true, `save_privkey_session` (session.rs:132-139) writes the key as bare hex to localStorage under "nostr_bbs_sk" with no encryption. The hex is the raw 32-byte secret key: both call sites pass `hex::encode(keypair.secret.as_bytes())` — `login_with_local_key` (mod.rs:439, nsec import) and `register_with_generated_key` (mod.rs:357-360, generated key). No mitigating encryption exists; the code itself documents this as a known "TRANSITIONAL PATH (audit C2/B8)" concern (session.rs:28-31). I searched for any encryption/wrapping at the call sites and found none. Note: the docstring at mod.rs:351 even falsely claims "never persisted to storage" immediately before calling save_privkey_session, reinforcing the finding. Passkey users are genuinely not affected (they re-derive via PRF and restore_session only restores pubkey for them), so the exposure is scoped to the local-key/nsec-import user class — but for those users the bare secret key is durably readable by any same-origin script (XSS) and survives tab close. Could not refute or find an already-present mitigation. Severity high is justified: durable at-rest exposure of a master signing key with remember-me defaulted ON.

confirmed.title: cargo-deny supply-chain policy never gates merges (continue-on-error + aggregator only checks fmt/wasm)
confirmed.evidence: deny job: `continue-on-error: true` (L166). ci-pass aggregator: `for result in "${{ needs.fmt.result }}" "${{ needs.wasm.result }}"; do if [[ "$result" != "success" ]]; then ... exit 1` (L203-210). deny/test/clippy results only echoed, never gated.
confirmed.recommendation: Move `deny` (and at minimum `test`) into the hard-gate loop in ci-pass, or remove `continue-on-error: true` from the deny job so a new advisory/license/source violation blocks the PR. Branch protection currently passes even if cargo-deny finds a critical advisory.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-reading .github/workflows/ci.yml confirms all three load-bearing claims. (1) The deny job carries `continue-on-error: true` at L166, so a cargo-deny failure does not fail that job. (2) The ci-pass aggregator's hard-gate loop at L203-205 iterates ONLY over `${{ needs.fmt.result }}` and `${{ needs.wasm.result }}`; only those two non-success states trigger `exit 1` (L206). (3) deny's result is merely echoed at L199 and never participates in the gate (same for clippy/test/doc). The comment at L201-202 explicitly states "clippy/test/doc/deny are advisory (continue-on-error) — visible above, non-blocking." Therefore cargo-deny's license/advisory/ban/source policy cannot block a PR via the single required ci-pass check. Partial mitigation exists in audit.yml: it runs `cargo audit --deny warnings` without continue-on-error on PRs, but (a) it only covers the ADVISORY subset, not cargo-deny's license/ban/source policy; (b) it triggers only on Cargo.lock path changes, so a new advisory against existing pinned deps or a license/source violation introduced without a lock change is not caught per-PR (only on the weekly schedule); and (c) it is itself not part of the ci-pass aggregator, so whether it gates merges depends on unverifiable external branch-protection config. The finding's specific claim — cargo-deny supply-chain policy never gates merges through the ci-pass aggregator — is accurate as written.

confirmed.title: audit.yml suppresses two advisories (RUSTSEC-2026-0097, RUSTSEC-2026-0173) absent from deny.toml and undocumented
confirmed.evidence: cargo audit ... --ignore RUSTSEC-2025-0141 --ignore RUSTSEC-2024-0384 --ignore RUSTSEC-2024-0436 --ignore RUSTSEC-2026-0097 --ignore RUSTSEC-2026-0173. deny.toml ignore=[] lists only the first three (deny.toml:13-15).
confirmed.recommendation: Make the two source-of-truth lists identical: either add RUSTSEC-2026-0097/0173 to deny.toml with a justification comment (crate, why, replacement plan) or drop them from audit.yml. Divergent ignore lists mean an advisory silenced in the weekly scan is invisible to the deny gate and vice versa.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The factual core is verifiable and real. audit.yml lines 44-49 ignore five advisories (RUSTSEC-2025-0141, 2024-0384, 2024-0436, 2026-0097, 2026-0173) while deny.toml lines 12-16 ignore only the first three. RUSTSEC-2026-0097 and 2026-0173 appear ONLY in audit.yml (confirmed by grep — no other file references them) and carry no justification comment, unlike each deny.toml entry which has a "# unmaintained: ..." note. So the lists do diverge and the two extras are undocumented. HOWEVER the finding's security framing/severity is overstated. (1) The auditor's claimed danger — "an advisory silenced in the weekly scan is invisible to the deny gate" — is backwards: the two divergent advisories are suppressed in FEWER places (only audit.yml), so cargo-deny still flags them; they are more visible, not hidden. No CVE is being secretly suppressed in a way that increases exposure. (2) cargo-deny is not actually a gate: the deny job in ci.yml line 166 runs with `continue-on-error: true`, so it never blocks anything regardless of these lists. This is therefore a configuration-hygiene / documentation-drift issue (two undocumented, inconsistent ignore entries), not a supply-chain vulnerability of medium severity.

confirmed.title: validate_config does not enforce zone id uniqueness — duplicate [[zones]] ids are silently shadowed (more-permissive duplicate can win)
confirmed.evidence: Zone validation only checks accent_hex: `for zone in &cfg.zones { if let Some(accent) = zone.accent_hex.as_deref() { ... } }`. No uniqueness check on zone.id. Consumer ZoneConfig::get (zone_config.rs:99-101) returns the FIRST match: `self.zones.iter().find(|z| z.id == id)`, so a duplicate id with weaker required_cohorts/visibility can shadow the intended one (or vice versa) with no error.
confirmed.recommendation: In validate_config, reject duplicate zone ids (mirror the calendar.shared_venues HashSet dedup already at lines 194-206), so an operator typo cannot silently produce two conflicting access rules for the same slug.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read validate.rs:208-218: the zone loop is `for zone in &cfg.zones { if let Some(accent) = zone.accent_hex.as_deref() { if !is_valid_hex_colour(accent) { return Err(...) } } }` — it only validates accent_hex and performs NO uniqueness check on zone.id. The calendar.shared_venues HashSet dedup the auditor cites as precedent does exist (validate.rs:194-206), confirming the asymmetry. The downstream find-first lookup is real and reachable: crates/nostr-bbs-relay-worker/src/zone_config.rs:100 `self.zones.iter().find(|z| z.id == id)` returns the first match, so a duplicate [[zones]] id placed earlier with weaker required_cohorts/visibility would silently shadow the intended zone for access-control decisions, with no validation error. One inaccuracy in the finding: it attributes the consumer to "zone_config.rs:99-101" implying the nostr-bbs-config crate, but ZoneConfig::get actually lives in the nostr-bbs-relay-worker crate (no such consumer exists in nostr-bbs-config). This is a path/crate mislabel, not a substantive error — the cited finding location (validate.rs in nostr-bbs-config) and the missing-uniqueness behavior are both correct. I confirm rather than refute because the core claim holds and the security-relevant code path exists. Corrected severity is low: triggering requires an operator self-inflicted config typo (no attacker-controlled input), though the access-control consequence justifies adding the guard.

confirmed.title: CI test job is non-blocking (continue-on-error) — no security test actually gates merges
confirmed.evidence: test job: `continue-on-error: true` with comment 'full-workspace suite has pre-existing failures'. ci-pass loop only checks `${{ needs.fmt.result }}` and `${{ needs.wasm.result }}` — 'Required gates (fmt, wasm32) passed. Advisory checks above are non-blocking.'
confirmed.recommendation: Make the `test` job a hard gate (remove continue-on-error) at least for security-critical crates, or add a dedicated required job `cargo test -p nostr-bbs-core -p nostr-bbs-relay-worker -p nostr-bbs-pod-worker --features test-exports` and add its result to the ci-pass required-result loop. Otherwise every security unit test below is decorative.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Re-read /home/user/nostr-rust-forum/.github/workflows/ci.yml. All of the auditor's factual claims hold verbatim. The `test` job (runs `cargo test --workspace`, line 101) carries `continue-on-error: true` at line 92 with comment "Advisory: full-workspace suite has pre-existing failures unrelated to deploy." The aggregator `ci-pass` (named "CI required", the intended branch-protection check per lines 177-181) only enforces fmt and wasm32: the loop at lines 203-205 iterates solely over `${{ needs.fmt.result }}` and `${{ needs.wasm.result }}`, exits 1 if either != success (206-209), and otherwise prints "Required gates (fmt, wasm32) passed. Advisory checks above are non-blocking." (211). `needs.test.result` is only echoed (line 196), never gated. clippy, test, doc, and deny are all continue-on-error and excluded from the required loop. The workspace does contain security-relevant tests that run only under this non-blocking job — e.g. SSRF tests in crates/nostr-bbs-preview-worker/src/ssrf.rs, whitelist/moderation/audit tests in nostr-bbs-relay-worker, and gift_wrap/nip04/cors/governance tests in nostr-bbs-core — so a regression in those would not fail the required check and would not block merge/deploy. The issue is real and reachable; I found no mitigating code. I downgrade severity from high to medium: this is a CI process / defense-in-depth gap (auditor's own category is "coverage"), not a directly exploitable code vulnerability — it removes a safety net rather than introducing a vuln.

confirmed.title: COUNT (NIP-45) read-path has zero auth/zone gating AND zero tests
confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: authentication state not validated on COUNT operations (existence/counts leak past auth-gate)
confirmed.evidence: handle_count(&self, ws, sub_id, filters){ let events = self.query_events(&filters).await; Self::send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len() as u64); } — no session_id param, so no authed_pubkey/zone scope; COUNT dispatch passes only `&ws`. REQ gets session_id and runs the full zone projector; COUNT does not.
confirmed.recommendation: First fix: route COUNT through the same zone/auth projector as REQ (pass session_id; reject or zero-out counts for zones the session can't read). Then add an integration/pure-fn test asserting a non-member COUNT over a Locked/Hidden-zone filter returns 0, and an unauth COUNT over kind-1059 is rejected — mirroring the REQ tests.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: Verified directly. handle_count(&self, ws, sub_id, filters) (nip_handlers.rs:976-984) takes no session_id and simply calls self.query_events(&filters).await then send_count(ws, sub_id, events.len()). query_events (storage.rs:241-302) is a raw D1 SELECT applying ONLY NIP-40 expiration filtering — no zone scope, no auth, no cohort projection. The COUNT dispatch (mod.rs:242-254) passes only &ws, unlike REQ (mod.rs:225) which passes session_id. handle_req (nip_handlers.rs:553-788) DOES gate: it rejects unauthenticated kind-1059 requests with 'auth-required' (lines 599-638) and runs the full zone projector (lines 678-763) that withholds Locked/Hidden-zone kind-42 content and Hidden-zone kind-40 defs from non-members, plus calendar projection. COUNT bypasses every one of these checks. This is a reachable existence/cardinality oracle: a non-member COUNT over a Locked/Hidden channel filter, or an unauth COUNT over kind-1059 #p:<victim>, returns the true count that REQ would withhold. Tests: grep for COUNT/handle_count/send_count in the crate's tests directory yields only an unrelated MAX_TAG_COUNT constant — zero coverage. Auditor's evidence and reasoning are accurate.

confirmed.title: REQ zone-read projector and async has_zone_access have no integration test (only pure sub-predicates tested)
confirmed.downstream: Relay zone-gating (A1-A3): read-path authorization bypass — REQ/COUNT may return events from zones the requester is not authorized to read
confirmed.evidence: handle_req computes `is_admin`, `access_pubkey`, and per-event `trust::has_zone_access(pk,&zone,&self.env)` / `zones.is_public_read(&zone)` / `defs_visible_to_nonmember` — none of this assembled decision path is covered. zone_config.rs tests only the leaf predicates; tests/ dir has no zone/has_zone_access/project_tier references (grep returned only MAX_TAG_COUNT).
confirmed.recommendation: Add test-exports for a pure `req_event_visible(zone, viewer_cohorts, is_admin, kind, defs_only) -> bool` extracted from handle_req's per-event branch, then unit-test: non-member sees Public content but not Locked/Hidden content; non-member sees Locked tile (def) but not Hidden tile; admin sees all. This closes the A1-A3 read-bypass class at the decision-assembly level, not just leaf predicates.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The coverage gap is real and accurately described. handle_req in nip_handlers.rs (lines 660-763) assembles the full REQ read-authorization decision: is_admin (664), access_pubkey (655-658), viewer_cohorts (672-675), and per-event branching that calls trust::has_zone_access (740), zones.is_public_read (741), and zones.defs_visible_to_nonmember (748), with a kind-40-vs-kind-42 split (745-756) and admin short-circuit (734). trust.rs has_zone_access/get_viewer_cohorts exist as cited (lines ~635-655, near 645). The zone_config.rs tests (lines 159-215) exercise ONLY the leaf predicates (is_public_read, cohorts_can_read, defs_visible_to_nonmember, cohorts_can_write) against an isolated ZoneConfig struct. A grep of the entire tests/ directory for has_zone_access|is_public_read|defs_visible_to_nonmember|handle_req|cohorts_can_read returns ZERO matches; nip_handlers_tests.rs references only MAX_TAG_COUNT (lines 44, 88), exactly as the auditor reported. So no test exercises the assembled decision (admin passthrough, unauth-as-non-member at 741, the kind-40 tile-vs-content branch). However, this is a coverage finding, not a demonstrated defect: the auditor did not show the assembled logic is wrong (on inspection it is deny-by-default and appears correct), only that it is untested. The downstream "read-path bypass" is therefore a latent regression risk, not a present vulnerability.

confirmed.title: Pod CORS can emit wildcard Allow-Origin with Allow-Credentials:true — no test forbids the combination
confirmed.downstream: Pod stored-XSS + CORS: CORS reflects Origin or uses wildcard '*' while allowing credentials
confirmed.evidence: cors_headers: `.unwrap_or_else(|_| "*".to_string()); headers.set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", &origin)` then loops POD_CORS_HEADERS which include `("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true")`. NIP-05 path additionally does `resp.headers().set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*")` right after applying credentialed cors. No test asserts they never co-occur.
confirmed.recommendation: Apply the downstream patch (drop Allow-Credentials when origin resolves to '*'), then add a unit test on cors_headers asserting: if Allow-Origin == '*' then Allow-Credentials header is absent; and a test on the NIP-05 response that it does not carry both. The credentialed-wildcard is the exploitable combination the downstream report flagged.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The finding's factual claims are all verified in the current code. In cors_headers (lib.rs:165-177), when EXPECTED_ORIGIN is unset the origin defaults to "*" (line 169: `.unwrap_or_else(|_| "*".to_string())`), is set as Access-Control-Allow-Origin (line 172), and the subsequent loop (lines 173-174) applies POD_CORS_HEADERS, which includes `("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true")` at cors.rs:55. So wildcard origin + credentials:true is emittable. The NIP-05 path (lib.rs:463-466) is worse and unconditional: it applies cors_headers(&env) (carrying Allow-Credentials:true) and then on line 466 forces `resp.headers().set("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "*")` regardless of EXPECTED_ORIGIN — guaranteeing the `*` + credentials:true combination on every NIP-05 response. No test forbids the combination: the only cors test asserting this header (cors.rs:112-114) asserts Allow-Credentials=="true" is PRESENT, and nothing anywhere asserts wildcard and credentials never co-occur (grep for Allow-Origin/Allow-Credentials in test files returned nothing). The coverage gap is real. HOWEVER, the severity framing overstates impact: per the Fetch spec, browsers reject a response combining Allow-Origin:* with Allow-Credentials:true for credentialed (credentials:'include') requests, so this specific combination is NOT a working credential-theft vector. The genuinely dangerous pattern — reflecting the request's Origin header with credentials:true — does NOT occur here; the origin value derives solely from the EXPECTED_ORIGIN env var or static "*"/NIP-98 fallbacks (lib.rs:166-169, 590-593), never from req.headers Origin. It is a spec-violating misconfiguration and a legitimate missing-test gap, but not the high-severity exploitable credentialed-wildcard the downstream report implies.

confirmed.title: No X-Content-Type-Options:nosniff anywhere in pod-worker, and no test enforces it
confirmed.downstream: Pod stored-XSS + CORS: user-controlled content served without nosniff/escaping
confirmed.evidence: grep for `nosniff`/`X-Content-Type-Options` in pod-worker/src/lib.rs returns 'No matches found'; only git.rs mentions it. User-controlled pod content (GET of a stored resource) is served without nosniff, enabling MIME-sniff-driven stored XSS.
confirmed.recommendation: Add `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` to the shared response builder (e.g. in add_ldp_headers or a central with_security_headers), then add a test asserting every GET resource response carries nosniff. This is the exact downstream-proposed patch and is currently both unimplemented and untested.
confirmed.verifier_rationale: The substantive claim holds. A case-insensitive grep for "nosniff"/"X-Content-Type-Options" across the entire repo returns zero matches, so the header is set nowhere. The resource GET path (lib.rs:870-957) fetches the stored R2 object, serves it with `obj_content_type` derived from the user-supplied stored content-type (`stored_content_type` at 876-879, set from the request Content-Type at PUT, lib.rs:645-649), and applies only `add_ldp_headers` (Link/Accept-Ranges/Updates-Via, lines 182-203), `add_wac_allow`, and `add_cache_control` — none of which emit nosniff. `cors_headers` (165-177) + `POD_CORS_HEADERS` (core/src/cors.rs:46-60) contain only Access-Control-* headers, no X-Content-Type-Options. No test references the header. So user-controlled pod content is served without nosniff, and indeed the content-type is echoed back verbatim with no restriction or Content-Disposition (only `text/html` literal in the codebase is the worker's own container listing at 858), so a user can store text/html and have it rendered — the absence of nosniff removes the defense-in-depth the finding flags. One evidence inaccuracy: the finding says "only git.rs mentions it," but git.rs contains no such mention either (grep finds none repo-wide); this does not affect the conclusion since the header's absence is the point. Note the deeper issue is content-type passthrough enabling stored HTML rendering; nosniff is the proposed mitigation, correctly identified.

confirmed.title: whitelist_tests.rs re-implements access-flag logic locally instead of testing src/whitelist.rs
confirmed.evidence: Test defines its OWN `fn compute_access_flags(is_admin, cohorts) -> (bool,bool,bool)` with hardcoded cohort lists ('home'|'lobby'|... ) under comment '(from src/whitelist.rs:113-138)'. It tests this copy, not the production function — assertions pass even if whitelist.rs diverges. Also uses a stale 3-boolean model, not the config-driven ZoneConfig.

confirmed.verifier_rationale:
The literal factual claim is TRUE and present: tests/whitelist_tests.rs:92-119 defines its own local `fn compute_access_flags(is_admin, cohorts) -> (bool,bool,bool)` with hardcoded cohort lists and the comment "(from src/whitelist.rs:113-138)". All the assertions at lines 121-235 exercise this duplicated copy. There is NO exported production `compute_access_flags`: the real logic is inline inside `handle_check_whitelist` (src/whitelist.rs:102-126), so the test genuinely cannot diverge-detect against production — it is duplicated/false-confidence coverage. So the coverage observation is confirmed.

HOWEVER the finding's severity and downstream framing are overstated and partly wrong. The 3-boolean has_home/has_members/has_private logic in whitelist.rs:102-126 only populates an INFORMATIONAL "access" field in the GET /api/check-whitelist JSON response (a client hint with "source":"relay"). It does NOT gate any reads. The actual relay zone-gating / read-path authorization (the claimed A1-A3 downstream) runs through src/trust.rs::has_zone_access (lines 645-656) and ZoneConfig::cohorts_can_read in src/zone_config.rs — a separate, config-driven code path that has its own real `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` (src/trust.rs:677+, src/zone_config.rs:163+). Therefore a divergence in the duplicated test logic could not produce a "read-path authorization bypass"; at worst it stales an informational JSON field. The "stale 3-boolean model vs config-driven ZoneConfig" remark also mischaracterizes the codebase, which already uses ZoneConfig for the security-relevant path. Net: real but minor test-hygiene/duplication issue, not a medium-severity authz-coverage gap.

confirmed.downstream: COUNT auth: authentication state not validated
confirmed.evidence: handle_auth enforces kind==22242, verify_event_strict, challenge_tag == session.challenge, and now.abs_diff(created_at) > 600 rejection, then sets authed_pubkey. nip_handlers_tests.rs covers only filter/kind/tag pure logic; no test exercises the challenge-binding or the 10-minute auth-window. The only timestamp test, timestamp_within_drift, uses MAX_TIMESTAMP_DRIFT = 604_800 (7 days, asserted at L98) — that is the general ingress drift (nip_handlers.rs:539), a DIFFERENT constant from the NIP-42 auth 600s window (L947). So neither the challenge-binding nor the 10-minute auth window is exercised by any test. authed_pubkey gates zone reads (nip_handlers.rs:596, broadcast.rs:55, session.rs:163), so the auditor's claim that a regression here is silent is correct.
