refuted_or_uncertain.title: Credentialed CORS with wildcard origin: Allow-Credentials:true hardcoded while Allow-Origin defaults to "*"
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale: The cited code exists exactly as described: cors.rs:55 hardcodes ("Access-Control-Allow-Credentials", "true") in POD_CORS_HEADERS, and cors_headers() (lib.rs:165-177) sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin to env EXPECTED_ORIGIN or literal "*" (line 169) while also emitting all POD_CORS_HEADERS including the credentials pair (lines 173-175). However, the claimed HIGH-severity exploit — credentialed wildcard CORS allowing cross-origin credentialed reads — is not achievable. Per the Fetch/CORS spec, browsers REJECT the combination `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` together with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`: a credentialed cross-origin request fails the CORS check and the response is never exposed to the attacker's script. So the unset-EXPECTED_ORIGIN case ("*" + credentials) breaks credentialed CORS rather than opening it. The genuinely dangerous pattern — reflecting the request's Origin header back with credentials — is NOT present: cors_headers() only ever uses a fixed env value or the literal string "*"; it never reads/reflects request.headers().get("Origin"). When EXPECTED_ORIGIN is set (production), origin is a single concrete value and credentials are correctly scoped (safe, valid config). I verified there is no origin-reflection path: the other origin-setting sites (lib.rs:466 sets "*" with no per-response credentials override; lib.rs:610 uses pay_cors_origin from EXPECTED_ORIGIN or a NIP-98-derived origin) likewise do not reflect an arbitrary attacker Origin. Thus the exploit narrative ("Pod stored-XSS + credentialed wildcard CORS") is refuted; what remains is a header-hygiene issue (emitting Allow-Credentials alongside "*" is a spec violation that should be cleaned up) with no browser-reachable security impact.

refuted_or_uncertain.title: Auto-hide moderation trips on 3 reports from any pubkeys — no reporter trust-tier or distinct-reporter gate (Sybil censorship)
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale:
The finding's central claim is false. It asserts auto-hide trips on "3 reports from any pubkeys" with "no reporter trust check" and that "process_report inserts every report unconditionally." In reality, kind-1984 reports ARE trust-gated before insertion. In nip_handlers.rs handle_event, lines 299-308 enforce: `if event.kind == KIND_REPORT_NIP56 && trust_level < TrustLevel::Member { send_ok(... "restricted: TL1+ required to report content"); return; }`. This return happens BEFORE save_event (line 459) and BEFORE process_report (line 481), so a sub-TL1 (non-admin) pubkey can never reach moderation::insert_report. The auditor's own recommendation ("check trust::get_trust_level >= TL1 before insert_report") is therefore already implemented one layer up. The docstring at moderation.rs:5-6 and :61 ("3+ pending reports ... from TL1+ users") is accurate, not misleading.

The only residual, narrower point with merit: the auto-hide query uses COUNT(*) (moderation.rs:94) rather than COUNT(DISTINCT reporter_pubkey), and the reports table (lib.rs:618-632) has only `report_event_id TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE` with `ON CONFLICT(report_event_id) DO NOTHING` — there is no UNIQUE(reporter_pubkey, reported_event_id). So a single TL1+ account could publish 3 distinct kind-1984 events targeting the same content and self-trip auto-hide. But this requires the actor to already hold TL1+ (a trust-earned tier via trust::check_promotion), not "any pubkey," which guts the Sybil-censorship premise. Additionally, auto-hide is a reversible soft-hide into hidden_events (not deletion), and reports still flow to admin review/resolution (handle_resolve_report). The finding as written — title, evidence, and "no trust check / inserts unconditionally" core — is factually incorrect, so it is refuted; the surviving distinct-reporter hardening is a low-severity defense-in-depth nit, not the medium-severity Sybil-censorship vuln described.

refuted_or_uncertain.title: Semantic /search endpoint is unauthenticated and zone-unaware — restricted-zone content discoverable via embeddings
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale:
The factual claim is correct — /search (lib.rs:557-559) calls handle_search with no NIP-98/zone check, and min_score defaults to 0.0 — but the finding's high-severity framing ("restricted-zone content discoverable", "read-path authorization bypass") is a misread of what the endpoint returns.

handle_search returns ONLY {id, distance, score} (lib.rs:303-316): a Nostr event id plus a cosine similarity score. No message content, author, channel, or zone is returned. The vector store (store.rs:20-23) holds only `(u64 label, [f32; DIM] embedding)` entries — content is never stored or served by this worker.

To obtain readable content, the client must hydrate event ids against the relay (global_search.rs:622 `hydrate_search_ids`), and the code comment at global_search.rs:621 states explicitly: "Missing ids (deleted or zone-withheld events) are simply absent from the returned map." Zone gating is therefore enforced at the relay read path during hydration, not bypassed. The broader zone-access subsystem (crates/nostr-bbs-forum-client/src/stores/zone_access.rs etc.) governs that path.

Nostr event ids are public, non-secret identifiers (SHA-256 of the signed event). The genuine residual issue is a metadata/oracle side-channel: an unauthenticated caller can submit a query embedding and learn similarity scores and event ids of indexed-but-zone-restricted events (i.e., infer "a message semantically near X exists" and obtain its id). That is a real but limited confidentiality/metadata concern — NOT content disclosure and NOT a read-path authorization bypass, since the content read path remains gated at the relay. The high rating rests on the incorrect premise that content is discoverable via /search.

refuted_or_uncertain.title: Vector store has no zone/cohort metadata and ingest silently drops the `channel` field — search cannot enforce zone authorization even if it wanted to
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale:
The structural facts are all confirmed: IngestEntry (lib.rs:113-117) deserializes only `id` + `embedding` and silently drops the client's `channel` field (search_client.rs:108); VectorStore (store.rs:20-23) stores only `entries: Vec<(u64,[f32;DIM])>` with no zone tag; and search() (store.rs:62-81) returns global nearest neighbors with no zone filter. The `/search` endpoint is also entirely unauthenticated (only `/ingest` requires NIP-98, lib.rs:386-404). However, the finding's SECURITY FRAMING — "read-path authorization bypass" of "Relay zone-gating (A1-A3)" at HIGH severity — is wrong, which is why I refute it.

The search worker is not, and was never designed to be, the read-authorization boundary. handle_search returns only `{id, distance, score}` (lib.rs:303-316) — event IDs and similarity scores, NEVER message content. The client (global_search.rs:280) even calls `search_similar(..., None)` passing no channel at search time at all. Actual content is then fetched by hydrate_search_ids (global_search.rs:622-643), which subscribes to the RELAY with an `ids` filter. The relay is the enforced security boundary: zone_access.rs:5-7 states "Zone enforcement is client-side (UX optimization); the relay is the source of truth per ADR-022," and zone_config.rs implements the real read gate (cohorts_can_read, is_public_read, lines 110-139). A non-member who obtains a locked-zone event ID from search still cannot read its content, because the relay refuses to serve gated events.

So there is no content authorization bypass. What actually remains is a lesser metadata/information-disclosure issue: unauthenticated search exposes the existence of event IDs in locked zones, their semantic similarity to arbitrary queries, and total vector counts. That is a real but low-severity leak, not the HIGH read-path authorization bypass claimed. The dropped `channel` field is forward-looking metadata, not an enforced control whose absence opens a content bypass. Corrected severity: low (info-disclosure of event IDs/scores), with the content read path correctly gated at the relay.

refuted_or_uncertain.title: Embeddings of `encrypted = true` zone posts are indexed in plaintext space and probeable anonymously
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale: The finding's core mechanism is a misread. It claims embeddings of "encrypted = true" zone posts are indexed in plaintext, implying the embedding defeats encryption that otherwise protects those posts. But the `encrypted` flag is inert. In the client store it is documented as "Client-side NIP-44 encryption flag (UX hint only)" and carries `#[allow(dead_code)]` (zones.rs:84-87); the relay copy says "relay only records it" (zone_config.rs:58). NIP-44 encryption is implemented only for DMs via NIP-59 gift wrap (dm/mod.rs), never for zone/channel posts. The single caller, channel.rs do_send_text (lines 477-549), builds a plaintext kind-42 UnsignedEvent (content at line 522), publishes it to the relay in plaintext (`relay.publish(&signed)`, line 530), then indexes that same plaintext (content_for_index, line 537). channel.rs does not appear among the files touching nip44/encrypt at all. So there is no "encrypted-zone post" whose protection is broken by the embedding — the post content is already published in plaintext to the relay regardless of the zone's `encrypted` setting. The premise that the embedding uniquely leaks otherwise-encrypted content is false. A genuinely adjacent concern exists (gated/locked zone content gets ingested into a search store whose /embed and /search calls pass no auth token — search_client.rs:45,68,81), but that is a different claim about zone read-gating via the search index, depends on whether the search worker enforces channel/zone scoping (outside the cited client code), and is not the "encryption defeated" issue this finding asserts. As written, the finding is a false positive.

refuted_or_uncertain.title: Quota owner-isolation and atomic check-and-reserve have only 2 trivial tests
refuted_or_uncertain.rationale: The finding's core security claim — that owner-to-pubkey mapping is "wholly untested" and that the recommended /pods/<A>/../<B>/ confusion test should be added — is factually wrong; those tests already exist. parse_pod_route (lib.rs:103) extracts owner_pubkey as a fixed-width 64-char hex segment via `rest.split_at(64)` with `is_ascii_hexdigit()` validation, so an owner's bytes cannot be attributed to another owner by construction (a `..` segment can only land in the remainder, never the pubkey). lib.rs has 9+ parse_pod_route tests: parse_pod_route_valid (1682) asserts `assert_eq!(pk, pubkey)` proving correct owner extraction; parse_pod_route_rejects_traversal (1782) asserts `/pods/{pubkey}/../etc` returns None — the exact "does not attribute to A" scenario the auditor recommends adding. Plus is_safe_resource_path_* tests (1743-1779) cover `..`, encoded dot (%2e), slash (%2f), backslash (%5c), and NUL. The only accurate observation is that quota.rs itself has 2 unit tests and the SQL reserve predicate `(used_bytes + ?2) <= limit_bytes` (quota.rs:112) is not unit-tested — but that is pure D1 SQL that cannot be unit-tested in WASM without a live database, and the logic is correct on inspection. The finding overstates a minor coverage gap as a medium owner-isolation risk while misrepresenting the actual, substantial test coverage of the owner-mapping path.

lens_summaries.summary: REQ enforces read authorization correctly (auth gate on kind-1059, mandatory #p injection, and a per-event zone-filtering loop covering channel and calendar kinds), but COUNT does NOT. handle_count is dispatched without session_id and calls query_events() directly, returning events.len() with zero auth/zone/cohort gating. query_events (storage.rs) and build_filter_conditions (filter.rs) are pure NIP-01 filter-to-SQL with no zone/cohort/auth predicate; ALL read-side authorization lives only in handle_req's post-query loop. Therefore an unauthenticated client can COUNT events in Locked/Hidden zones, kind-1059 sealed DMs for any pubkey, and gated calendar events — leaking exact existence/counts that REQ correctly withholds. The downstream A1-A3 "REQ returns events from unauthorized zones" finding is MITIGATED on the current REQ path, but the COUNT-auth leak is PRESENT and reachable.

The WoT admin CRUD API (status/set-referente/refresh/override) IS wired and reachable — lib.rs:396-411 routes 5 admin-gated endpoints into wot.rs. But the actual trust ENFORCEMENT is dead: wot::is_allowed_by_wot (wot.rs:431), documented as "used from webauthn.rs when wot_enabled = 1", is never called anywhere in the workspace. The registration path register_verify (webauthn.rs:827-1028) writes credentials to D1 (line 1000-1016) with NO WoT check. The companion invite-code bypass invites::consume_for_registration (invites.rs:608), documented identically as called from register_verify, is also never called, and the invite_code field on RegisterVerifyBody (webauthn.rs:177) is never consumed. Net effect: an admin can set wot_enabled=1 and configure a referente follow-list believing registration is gated, but ANY pubkey can register regardless. This is a silently-bypassed trust gate, not merely unused code. The fix is to WIRE it (add the is_allowed_by_wot/consume_for_registration check into register_verify before the D1 insert), not delete it — deletion would remove a configured-but-non-functional security control without restoring the intended behavior, and the admin UI/DB state would still advertise WoT as active.

The downstream "Pod stored-XSS + CORS" item is CONFIRMED PRESENT and the proposed patch (add nosniff; drop credentialed wildcard CORS) is genuinely needed and NOT yet applied. (1) Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true is hardcoded in core/src/cors.rs POD_CORS_HEADERS and applied to every pod response via cors_headers(), while Access-Control-Allow-Origin defaults to the literal "*" (lib.rs:169) and the NIP-05 endpoint explicitly forces Origin: "*" (lib.rs:466) — a definitively invalid credentialed-wildcard combination. (2) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff is set nowhere in any crate (zero matches). (3) Stored content is served as attacker-chosen Content-Type: the client-supplied Content-Type is stored verbatim on PUT/POST (lib.rs:1015/1066/1122) and content_negotiation::negotiate returns text/html whenever the request sends Accept: text/html (content_negotiation.rs:84-86) — even for non-HTML stored types — with no HTML escaping or sanitization, yielding reachable same-origin stored XSS. The /profile/card path is unconditionally served as text/html (lib.rs:858) and its only "validation" (validate_webid_html, lib.rs:1518) checks for a JSON-LD script block and does NOT sanitize surrounding markup. One nuance vs the downstream wording: CORS origin is read from the EXPECTED_ORIGIN env var, not reflected from the request Origin header — but the wildcard default plus hardcoded credentials is the real, exploitable defect.

All three downstream items were verified against current pod-worker code. (a) Container listing is safely gated: list_container (container.rs:16) is reachable only via the GET branch (lib.rs:820), which sits downstream of the deny-by-default WAC gate (lib.rs:796-809) — an unauthorized party cannot enumerate a container they lack acl:Read on; find_effective_acl returns None=>deny when no ACL grants access. (b) NIP-05 /.well-known/nostr.json (lib.rs:426-469) is a designed public name->pubkey lookup (the NIP-05 spec is inherently public), but it is a clean existence oracle (200+JSON for hit vs 404 "Name not found" for miss); mitigated by per-IP rate limiting (60/min) that is explicitly fail-open on KV degradation. (c) Quota attribution is correct: the quota key passed to check_and_reserve_d1 is always owner_pubkey parsed from the URL path /pods/{pubkey}/ (parse_pod_route, lib.rs:103/619), and quota_usage.pubkey is PRIMARY KEY (payments.rs:87) — no cross-owner charging via the key binding. However, a real, reachable secondary issue exists: the provisioned inbox ACL grants acl:AuthenticatedAgent Append (provision.rs:273-288), so any authenticated third party can POST to another owner's /inbox/ and consume the OWNER's quota (charged to owner_pubkey at lib.rs:1107) — a quota-exhaustion DoS chargeable to the victim owner.

The nostr-bbs-core crypto surface is well-constructed and defensively coded. event.rs verify_event_strict always recomputes the event ID from canonical NIP-01 serialization (never trusts the client id) and verifies the BIP-340 Schnorr signature over that recomputed id, with validated pubkey-point and signature decoding — the relay calls this on every inbound EVENT (nip_handlers.rs:164). NIP-44 v2 and NIP-04 are fully delegated to the audited rust-nostr 0.44 crate (no hand-rolled nonce/IV/MAC handling), and NIP-98 implements timestamp tolerance, recomputed-id binding, exact-URL/case-insensitive-method binding, payload-hash binding, and an atomic replay store keyed on the canonical id. did:nostr validation delegates to solid_pod_rs with strict 64-hex + on-curve checks. The two real (low-severity) issues are: (1) verify_nip98/verify_token/verify_token_at expose a stateless verification path with NO replay protection, so a downstream caller that picks the wrong function gets no replay defense and the doc comment never warns about it; and (2) a u64->u16 kind truncation in to_upstream_builder that diverges from the kit's own u64 canonical serializer for kinds >65535 (a signing-correctness/interop bug, not a verification bypass — the verifier is internally self-consistent). No nonce/IV reuse, no missing constant-time compares in kit-owned code (MAC/sig comparison happens inside rust-nostr/k256), no error path that bypasses verification, and verification code paths are panic-free on attacker-controlled input.

The relay-worker's REQ read path is well-gated (zone filtering, deny-by-default calendar projection, kind-1059 #p injection, auth checks), but two sibling paths that serve the SAME events skip that gating entirely. handle_count (nip_handlers.rs:976) calls query_events directly with no auth, no zone filter, no calendar projection, and no subscription-limit check — so COUNT leaks existence/counts of Locked/Hidden-zone channel content and zone-private calendar events to any unauthenticated client (this is the downstream "COUNT auth" item, CONFIRMED present). broadcast_event (broadcast.rs:41) applies zone gating ONLY to kind-1059; every other zone-scoped kind (kind-42 Locked/Hidden content, calendar 31922/31923/31925) is pushed live to any subscriber whose plain filter matches, bypassing the careful handle_req projection — a real cross-tier read leak on the live-push path. Separately, NIP-11 advertises max_filters:10 but no code enforces it, and each filter is a separate D1 query in query_events, giving an unauthenticated COUNT/REQ filter-fan-out amplification DoS. NIP-09 deletion authz, the NIP-42 AUTH challenge (CSPRNG + per-session match), the calendar projector (deny-by-default, RSVP spoof-resistant target resolution), gift-wrap recipient broadcast gating, and the query LIMIT cap are all correctly implemented (verified-mitigated).

Audited crates/nostr-bbs-pod-worker beyond CORS/container/quota: WAC deny-by-default (acl.rs), the git stub path (git.rs), JSON-Patch write path (patch.rs), remote_storage.rs, provision.rs, storage/cf_backend.rs, and path-traversal across all of them. Core authorization is sound: NIP-98 auth with D1 atomic replay protection gates writes, WAC is deny-by-default (no ACL => deny-all), .acl writes are coerced to require acl:Control (preventing acl:Write->Control escalation), R2 sidecars are authoritative over a legacy KV fallback (delegation-masking fix is real and unit-tested), path traversal is blocked at parse_pod_route via is_safe_resource_path (literal + percent-encoded .. / \ NUL) and re-validated on the Slug-derived child path, the git smart-HTTP routes are inert 501 stubs (no subprocess, so no command/arg injection surface), and .git/ internals are 403'd. remote_storage.rs makes NO outbound requests, so there is no SSRF there. quota is keyed to the route owner_pubkey atomically (no cross-owner leak in the write path). The real, reachable issues are content-handling: (1) NO X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on ANY pod response, and (2) content_negotiation::negotiate returns text/html for ANY stored resource when the client sends Accept: text/html — together a concrete stored-XSS vector for attacker-uploaded content served from world-readable containers. Plus a low-severity DID-shape validation gap in the delegation shortcut.

Audited nostr-bbs-auth-worker's authn/authz surface (webauthn.rs, crypto.rs, auth.rs, devices.rs, invites.rs, username.rs, admin.rs/admins.rs, http.rs, did.rs, pod.rs) plus the core nip98.rs it delegates to. The WebAuthn ceremony, NIP-98 verification (with D1-backed replay protection), login-enumeration hardening, invite forgery/reuse defenses, username squatting/homoglyph defenses, device ownership scoping, and admin authz gating are all genuinely well-implemented and match the security-model intent. The one real, reachable defect is that Web-of-Trust registration gating is dead code: register_verify declares an inviteCode field and the crate ships is_allowed_by_wot() + consume_for_registration(), but NOTHING calls them — so enabling wot_enabled=1 does not actually gate passkey registration. This both confirms the downstream "WoT dead code" finding AND means the invite-gated-registration feature is silently inert. Two lower-severity notes: admin authz never case-normalizes the NIP-98 pubkey before comparison (robustness, not an exploit), and a username claim auto-grants relay whitelist "members" cohort (a deliberate but privilege-granting cross-D1 write). The downstream NIP-05 enumeration concern maps to /api/username/check, which is an intentional open availability oracle.

The preview worker's SSRF guard (crates/nostr-bbs-preview-worker/src/ssrf.rs) is reasonably hardened for the IPv4 surface and correctly re-validates redirects on every hop, blocks non-http schemes, userinfo, non-standard ports, .local/.localhost, integer/hex/octal IPv4 (via url-crate WHATWG host normalization to canonical dotted-decimal), and the common IPv6 ranges (::1, fc/fd ULA, fe80 link-local, ::ffff: mapped). I empirically verified url 2.5.x normalization with a harness. However I found two genuine issues. (1) HIGH/structural: the egress allowlist (set_allowlist / PREVIEW_ALLOWED_HOSTS) is the documented "authoritative" DNS-rebinding mitigation, but set_allowlist() is never called anywhere in the repo and the lazy std::env fallback does not work in the CF Workers WASM runtime (vars come via the JS Env object, not process env) — so in production the allowlist is always empty and the worker runs in rebind-vulnerable denylist-only mode by construction. (2) MEDIUM: the IPv6 denylist matches by textual prefix rather than parsing the address, missing NAT64 (64:ff9b::/96), IPv4-compatible (::a.b.c.d), 6to4 (2002::/16), and deprecated site-local (fec0::/10) forms that embed loopback/metadata (e.g. http://[64:ff9b::7f00:1]/ and http://[::a9fe:a9fe]/ are NOT blocked). DNS rebinding for plain public hostnames remains an accepted/documented residual risk in this runtime. The downstream pod CORS/nosniff concern does not apply to this worker (no credentialed CORS, no wildcard).

The forum-client's XSS posture is strong: every user-content inner_html sink (MentionText, message_input preview) routes through utils/sanitize.rs, which runs comrak with unsafe_=false (strips ALL raw HTML) plus tagfilter. I empirically confirmed (built against comrak 0.38.0, the pinned version) that this config also neutralizes javascript:/data:/vbscript: and entity-encoded URL schemes in markdown links (href/src rewritten to ""), closing the one gap the test suite does not cover. The other inner_html sinks (recovery_sheet.rs, settings.rs QR codes) render in-WASM-generated SVG from controlled data, not user markup. Avatar/media/link-preview URL sinks are http(s)-scheme-validated or rendered in passive contexts with Leptos auto-escaping. DM plaintext is rendered as escaped text and held in-memory only (no plaintext-at-rest). The downstream "missing throwaway-key warnings" item is mitigated (NsecBackup + RecoverySheet show strong bearer-credential warnings; connect.rs strips the nsec fragment from history before import). The one material finding is a SECRETS issue, not XSS: local-key/imported-nsec sessions persist the raw 32-byte Schnorr master private key as hex in localStorage by default (remember-me on), so any same-origin script — a future XSS, a malicious dependency, or a hostile browser extension — can exfiltrate the full account key. Passkey sessions correctly never persist. This raises the blast radius of any single XSS bug from session-scoped to full account takeover.

The supply-chain and robustness posture is mostly sound but undermined by CI gating gaps. All 15 `unsafe` occurrences are sound: test-only no-op Wakers (signer.rs/gift_wrap.rs/nip98.rs), wasm32-only `unsafe impl Send/Sync` wrappers backed by `SendWrapper` runtime single-thread enforcement (forum-client), an in-place hex zeroize over owned mutable bytes (auth/session.rs:326), and one English-word "unsafe" in an acl.rs comment (not Rust unsafe). Worker request-handling paths are far less panic-prone than the raw counts suggest: filtering out `#[cfg(test)]` modules leaves essentially zero attacker-reachable panics in pod-worker (1 guarded unwrap at acl.rs:406), and the relay/auth panics that remain are either statically infallible (`try_into` on fixed-size slices), validated upstream (moderation.rs:296 `unreachable!` is preceded by an exhaustive allow-list check at line 261 and is admin-gated), or infra-conditional rather than input-conditional (getrandom on the CSPRNG). `verify_event_strict` (event.rs:205) is fully Result-based on attacker input, confirming the downstream note that the relay validates inbound EVENTs. The real risks are process-level: (1) CI's `deny`, `test`, and `clippy` jobs are all `continue-on-error` and the `ci-pass` aggregator only hard-gates `fmt` and `wasm`, so cargo-deny never blocks a merge; (2) audit.yml ignores TWO advisories (RUSTSEC-2026-0097, RUSTSEC-2026-0173) that deny.toml does not list and does not document; (3) none of the ignored advisories carry an expiry/review date.

The semantic search worker is the dominant risk in this scope. Its /search and /embed endpoints are completely unauthenticated and zone-unaware: the in-memory vector store keys entries only on (label, embedding) with no zone/cohort metadata, and the ingest path silently discards the `channel` field clients send (lib.rs IngestEntry deserializes only id+embedding). Consequently any anonymous caller can POST a query and receive the event IDs (and similarity scores) of the nearest indexed posts irrespective of the zone they live in — including locked/hidden and `encrypted=true` zones whose plaintext is embedded client-side at ingest. This is a present-and-reachable read-path authorization bypass that parallels the downstream relay zone-gating finding, but in a worker that has no zone-gating at all. The honest client re-hydrates bodies via the relay (so withheld content bodies are not returned by the UI), but direct API callers still get IDs/scores and the embeddings themselves leak semantic content. The config crate enforces deny-by-default well for zone *visibility* (serde lowercase enum fails closed on typos; empty cohorts + non-public = admin-only) and admin/governance pubkeys are hex-checked, but validate_config never checks zone `id` uniqueness (duplicate ids are silently shadowed by ZoneConfig::get's first-match) and never validates mesh.peer_relays URLs (no wss:// enforcement) despite enforcing wss:// on relay.url and governance.relay_url. The mesh crate is scaffold-only: traits exist but no concrete trust enforcement, and MeshTransport::broadcast_kind30033 does not assert session.authenticated (enforcement deferred to unimplemented implementors). Ingest is correctly admin-gated via NIP-98 + D1 replay protection.

Security-critical paths in nostr-rust-forum have uneven test coverage. Strongly covered (with direct unit tests): SSRF guard (~30 tests in preview-worker/ssrf.rs), NIP-98 replay/timestamp (nip98.rs), Schnorr signature verification (event.rs verify_event_strict), WAC/ACL deny-by-default (pod-worker/acl.rs, ~44 tests), and the pure zone-policy predicates (zone_config.rs cohorts_can_read/is_public_read). However, the most exploitable gaps the downstream report flagged are confirmed present AND untested: (1) COUNT (NIP-45) has NO auth/zone gating and NO test — handle_count reuses raw query_events; (2) the REQ read-path zone projector and async has_zone_access are exercised by no integration test (only their pure sub-predicates are); (3) pod CORS can emit wildcard Allow-Origin together with Allow-Credentials:true and no test forbids that combination; (4) nosniff is absent and untested; (5) quota owner-isolation/atomic check-and-reserve has only 2 trivial tests. Compounding everything: the CI `test` job is continue-on-error, so none of these tests actually gate merges.
